1: A revelation of God: 'answer me when I call, O God of my righteousness! You have given me relief when I was in distress. Be gracious to me and hear my prayer!' The evening cry to God — the God of the psalmist's righteousness (Elohei tzidqi) — reveals the divine character as the one who gives relief in distress. Christ, the perfectly righteous one, makes this cry his own in Gethsemane and on the cross ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' Psalms 22:1) and by entering that distress opens the relief to those who have no righteousness of their own.
2: A theme: 'O men, how long shall my honor be turned into shame? How long will you love vain words and seek after lies?' The psalmist's honor turned to shame — the reversal that the righteous experience in a world that honors vanity. Christ's honor was supremely turned to shame at the crucifixion (the purple robe of mockery, the crown of thorns, the placard) — and the resurrection reversed the reversal: 'God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:9).
3: A theme: 'know that the LORD has set apart the godly for himself; the LORD hears when I call to him.' The divine setting-apart of the chasid (godly/loyal one) prefigures Christ's unique setting-apart: the holy one of God whom demons recognized (Mark 1:24), anointed and set apart for the specific mission of bearing the world's sin. The Father's hearing of the Son's prayers (John 11:41-42: 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me') is the covenant assurance of Psalms 4:3 realized perfectly.
4: A revelation of God: 'be angry, and do not sin; ponder in your own hearts on your beds, and be silent.' Paul quotes this verse in Ephesians 4:26 as guidance for Christian anger in the community. The revelation here is of a God who acknowledges righteous anger while demanding its discipline — a character fully expressed in Christ who 'drove out those who sold and those who bought' (Mark 11:15) in righteous anger while never sinning. Anger and holiness coexist in Christ as in no other.
5: A theme: 'offer right sacrifices, and put your trust in the LORD.' The right sacrifices (zivchei tzedek) — the covenant worship that is both externally correct and internally aligned with trust. Christ both fulfills the sacrificial system (Hebrews 10:5-10: 'a body you have prepared for me') and abolishes the need for further offering by being himself the right sacrifice. The trust (bitchu) in YHWH is simultaneously the posture Ps 4 calls for and what the sacrifice of Christ enables — access to trust the Father.
6: A theme: 'there are many who say, Who will show us some good? Lift up the light of your face upon us, O LORD!' The longing for the face of YHWH to shine — the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26) — is the universal human longing for divine favor. Christ is the answer to this prayer: 'the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ' (2 Corinthians 4:6). The divine face the world longed to see became visible in the incarnation.
7: A revelation of God: 'you have put more joy in my heart than they have when their grain and wine abound.' The covenant joy that surpasses material prosperity — the joy that Christ imparts is specifically this kind: 'that my joy may be in you, and that your joy may be full' (John 15:11). The abundance of grain and wine (the covenant blessings of earth) yields to the joy of divine presence. The Last Supper itself holds the tension: earthly bread and wine become the sign of the greater covenant joy.
8: A theme: 'in peace I will both lie down and sleep; for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety.' The peace that enables sleep — Christ who 'slept' in the boat (Mark 4:38) with absolute peace amid the storm is the model of this trust. The deeper application: the peace that Christ gives is 'not as the world gives' (John 14:27) — it holds even in the face of crucifixion. And his lying down in death became the sleep from which YHWH alone could raise him, making him dwell in ultimate safety.
